title: toxicodendron radicans subsp. diversiloba
intro: Toxicodendron radicans subsp. diversiloba, commonly known as poison oak, is a subspecies of the poison ivy plant native to North America, characterized by its three-leafed arrangement and shiny green leaves. The plant contains urushiol, a resinous oil that causes allergic reactions, including skin irritation and blisters, upon contact. However, when used in medicinal preparations, poison oak has been traditionally employed to treat various health conditions, such as fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions, due to its anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties. Herbal preparations of poison oak, including tinctures and infusions, are typically used to treat these conditions, although caution is advised due to the plant's potential for skin irritation.
